$$\therefore \quad x \geq \frac{M g}{k}=\frac{g}{4 \pi^2}=\frac{10}{4 \times 10}=0.25 \mathrm{~m}$$

content: A simple pendulum of length '$$l$$' and a bob of mass '$$\mathrm{m}$$' is executing S.H.M. of small amplitude '$$A$$'. The maximum tension in the string will be ($$\mathrm{g}=$$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Tension in the string is given as,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}^{\prime}=\mathrm{mg} \cos \theta+\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{l} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_{\max }^{\prime}=\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{l} \\ \end{aligned}$$

Now, time period $$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$$

$$\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}}{l}}$$

Maximum Velocity is given as $$\mathrm{v}_{\max }=A \omega$$

Substituting the values

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{T}_{\max }^{\prime} & =\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{A} \omega)^2}{l} \\ & =\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\frac{\mathrm{mA}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{l}}{l} \\ & =\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{mA}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{l^2} \\ & =\mathrm{mg}\left(1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{l}\right)^2\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The displacement of a particle executing S.H.M. is $$x=\mathrm{a} \sin (\omega t-\phi)$$. Velocity of the particle at time $$\mathrm{t}=\frac{\phi}{\omega}$$ is $$\left(\cos 0^{\circ}=1\right)$$
explanation:
To calculate the velocity of a particle in simple harmonic motion (S.H.M.), we use the derivative of the displacement function with respect to time. The displacement function given is:

$$x = a \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

The velocity $$v$$ is the first derivative of displacement $$x$$ with time $$t$$:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

Let's compute the derivative:

$$v = \frac{d}{dt} \left[ a \sin(\omega t - \phi) \right]$$ $$v = a \cos(\omega t - \phi) \cdot \frac{d}{dt} (\omega t - \phi)$$ $$v = a \cos(\omega t - \phi) \cdot \omega$$ $$v = a \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

We need to calculate this velocity at time $$t = \frac{\phi}{\omega}$$:

$$v = a \omega \cos\left(\omega \cdot \frac{\phi}{\omega} - \phi \right)$$ $$v = a \omega \cos(\phi - \phi)$$ $$v = a \omega \cos(0^{\circ})$$ $$v = a \omega \cdot 1$$ $$v = a \omega$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B:

$$a \omega$$

content: The bob of simple pendulum of length '$$L$$' is released from a position of small angular displacement $$\theta$$. Its linear displacement at time '$$\mathrm{t}$$' is ( $$\mathrm{g}=$$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Equation for displacement for a particle performing S.H.M. is,

$$y=A \cos \omega t=L \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{T} \times t\right)$$

But time period of a simple pendulum,

$$\begin{aligned} T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \\ \therefore \quad y & =L \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}} \times t\right)=L \cos \left(\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} \times t\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The linear displacement is,

$$s=y \theta=L \theta \cos \left[\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} \times t\right]$$

content: Under the influence of force $$F_1$$ the body oscillates with a period $$T_1$$ and due to another force $$F_2$$ body oscillates with period $$T_2$$. If both forces acts simultaneously, then the resultant period is (consider displacement is same in all three cases)
explanation:
Time period for the force $$F_1=T_1$$

Time period for the force $$F_2=T_2$$

If both forces acts simultaneously then the resultant force is $$F$$,

$$F=F_1+F_2$$

Let, time period for the force $$F$$ be $$T$$

Now, $$F=F_1+F_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & m \omega^2 y=m \omega_1^2 y+m \omega_2^2 y \quad\left(\because F=m \omega^2 y\right) \\ & \omega^2=\omega_1^2+\omega_2^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right)^2=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T_1}\right)^2+\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T_2}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{T^2}=\frac{1}{T_1^2}+\frac{1}{T_2^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{T^2}=\frac{T_2^2+T_1^2}{T_1^2 T_2^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad T^2=\frac{T_1^2 T_2^2}{T_1^2+T_2^2} \Rightarrow T=\sqrt{\frac{T_1^2 T_2^2}{T_1^2+T_2^2}} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: A mass $$M$$ is suspended from a light spring. An additional mass $$M_1$$ added extends the spring further by a distance $$x$$. Now, the combined mass will oscillate on the spring with period $$T=$$
explanation:
Time period of oscillation when mass $$M$$ hangs by spring of spring constant $$k$$ is given by,

$$T=2 \pi \sqrt{M / k}$$

From Hooke's law, $$k x=m g$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{k}=\frac{x}{m g}$$

When mass $$M_1$$ is added to the system

$$T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\left(M_1+M\right)}{k}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{x\left(M_1+M\right)}{m g}}$$

content: A body of mass $$0.04 \mathrm{~kg}$$ executes simple harmonic motion (SHM) about $$\mathrm{x}=0$$ under the influence of force $$\mathrm{F}$$ as shown in graph. The period of
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{k}=400 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m} \\\\ & \mathrm{m}=0.04 \end{aligned}$$

From the graph,

$$\mathrm{K}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{8}{2}=4$$

From $$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{K}}}$$,

we get

$$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{0.04}{4}}=0.2 \pi \mathrm{s}$$

content: A simple pendulum has a time period '$$T$$' in air. Its time period when it is completely immersed in a liquid of density one eighth the density of the material of bob is
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum $$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{\mathrm{~g}}}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{g}}}$$

Net downward force acting on the bob inside the liquid $$=$$ Weight of bob $$-$$ Upthrust

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{Vpg}-\mathrm{V} \frac{\rho}{\rho} \mathrm{g}=\frac{7}{8} \mathrm{~V} \rho \mathrm{g}$$

The value of $$g$$ inside the liquid will be $$\frac{g}{8}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Time period in liquid $$\mathrm{T}_1=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{\frac{l}{7}}{8}} =\sqrt{\frac{8}{7}} \mathrm{~T}$$

content: In a stationary lift, time period of a simple pendulum is '$$\mathrm{T}$$'. The lift starts accelerating downwards with acceleration $$\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{4}\right)$$, then the time period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
Time period of pendulum: $$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

When lift is accelerated downward with acceleration $$\frac{\mathrm{g}}{4}$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & g=g-\frac{g}{4} \\ & g=\frac{3 g}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore\quad$$ New Time period will be

$$\begin{aligned} & T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4 L}{3 g}} \\ & T_1=2 \pi \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \\ & T_1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} T \end{aligned}$$

content:
A particle starts from mean position and performs S.H.M. with period 4 second. At what time its kinetic energy is $$50 \%$$ of total energy?

$$\left(\cos 45^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$
explanation:
The total energy is given by $$T \cdot E=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kA}^2$$

Kinetic energy is given by $$\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2\right)$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { K.E }=\frac{1}{2} P . E \quad \text{...(given)}\\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2} k\left(A^2-x^2\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} k A^2\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \left(A^2-x^2\right)=\frac{1}{2} A^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(A^2-x^2\right)=\frac{1}{2} A^2 \\ \therefore \quad & x=\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}$$

The equation of displacement in SHM is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\sqrt{2}}=\mathrm{A} \sin \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{t}}{4} \quad ....(\because \text{T}=\text{4 sec})\\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sin \frac{\pi \mathrm{t}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\pi \mathrm{t}}{2}=\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \quad \frac{\pi \mathrm{t}}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=0.5 \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$$

content: A simple pendulum performs simple harmonic motion about $$\mathrm{x}=0$$ with an amplitude '$$\mathrm{a}$$' and time period '$$T$$'. The speed of the pendulum at $$x=\frac{a}{2}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{v} & =\omega \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{x}^2} \\ & \text { At } \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}, \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{v} & =\omega \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2-\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4}} \\ & =\omega \frac{\sqrt{3 \mathrm{a}}}{2} \\ & =\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}}{2} \quad .....\left(\because \omega=\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)\\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{v} & =\frac{\pi \mathrm{a} \sqrt{3}}{\mathrm{~T}} \end{aligned}$$

content: Four massless springs whose force constants are $$2 \mathrm{~K}, 2 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}$$ and $$2 \mathrm{~K}$$ respectively are attached to a mass $$\mathrm{M}$$ kept on a frictionless plane as shown in figure, If mass $$M$$ is displaced in horizontal direction then frequency of oscillating system is
explanation:
On the right hand side of the block, springs are connected in parallel

$$\therefore$$ Their effective spring constant is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_1=\mathrm{K}+2 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \mathrm{~K}_1=3 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$$

On the left hand side of the block, springs are connected in series.

$$\therefore$$ Their effective spring constant is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_2}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~K}}+\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~K}} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~K}_2=\mathrm{K} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Effective spring constant of the system is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \mathrm{K}_E=3 \mathrm{~K}+\mathrm{K}=4 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \therefore \quad \omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_E}{M}}=\sqrt{\frac{4 K}{M}} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{4 K}{M}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The upper end of the spring is fixed and a mass '$$m$$' is attached to its lower end. When mass is slightly pulled down and released, it oscillates with time period 3 second. If mass '$$\mathrm{m}$$' is increased by $$1 \mathrm{~kg}$$, the time period becomes 5 second. The value of '$$\mathrm{m}$$' is (mass of spring is negligible)
explanation:
The formula for the time period of a spring mass system is $$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

For mass $$\mathrm{m}+1, \mathrm{~T}^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}+1}{\mathrm{k}}}$$

Taking the ratio,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}^{\prime}} & =\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}}}}{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}+1}{\mathrm{k}}}} \\ \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}^{\prime}} & =\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}+1}} \\ \frac{3}{5} & =\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}+1}} \\ \frac{9}{25} & =\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}+1} \\ \therefore \quad 9 \mathrm{~m} & +9=25 \mathrm{~m} \\ \therefore \quad 16 \mathrm{~m} & =9 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{m} & =\frac{9}{16} \mathrm{~kg} \end{aligned}$$

content: For a particle executing S.H.M., its potential energy is 8 times its kinetic energy at certain displacement '$$x$$' from the mean position. If '$$A$$' is the amplitude of S.H.M the value of '$$x$$' is
explanation:
Potential energy: $$U=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2$$ and

Kinetic energy: $$K=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(A^2-x^2\right)$$

Given: Potential energy $$=8 \times$$ Kinetic energy

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{U}=8 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2=8 \times \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(A^2-x^2\right) \\ & x^2=8 A^2-8 x^2 \\ & 9 x^2=8 A^2 \\ & x^2=\frac{8 A^2}{9} \\ & x=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} A}{3} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The time period of a simple pendulum inside a stationary lift is '$$T$$'. When the lift starts accelerating upwards with an acceleration $$\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{3}\right)$$, the time period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
Time period of a simple pendulum is $$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{l}{\mathrm{a}}\right)}$$

In stationary lift, the value of acceleration is $$\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g}$$

$$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{l}{\mathrm{~g}}\right)}$$

When the lift is accelerating in an upward direction, there is a pseudo force acting in a downward direction.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{ma}=\mathrm{mg}+\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{3} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g}+\frac{\mathrm{g}}{3} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{a}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~g}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Period for a pendulum in accelerating lift is

$$\begin{aligned} \quad \mathrm{T}^{\prime} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{\mathrm{a}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{3 l}{4 \mathrm{~g}}} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{T}^{\prime} & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\left(2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{\mathrm{~g}}}\right) \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{T}^{\prime} & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~T} \end{aligned}$$

content: A body is executing a linear S.H.M. Its potential energies at the displacement '$$\mathrm{x}$$' and '$$\mathrm{y}$$' are '$$\mathrm{E}_1$$' and '$$E_2$$' respectively. Its potential energy at displacement $$(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})$$ will be
explanation:
We know,

Potential Energy $$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Kx}^2$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}_1=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Kx}^2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{E}_1}{\mathrm{~K}}}$$ .... (i)

and

$$E_2=\frac{1}{2} K y^2 \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{\frac{2 E_2}{K}}$$ ..... (ii)

Given, total displacement $$=(x+y)$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Potential energy at displacement $$(x+y)$$,

$$\mathrm{E}$$ is $$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~K}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} K\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_1}{K}}+\sqrt{\frac{2 E_2}{K}}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} K\left[\frac{2 E_1}{K}+\frac{2 E_2}{K}+2\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_1}{K}}\right)\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_2}{K}}\right)\right] \\ & =\left(\sqrt{E_1}+\sqrt{E_2}\right)^2=\left(E_1+E_2+2 \sqrt{E_1 E_2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: A simple harmonic progressive wave is represented by $$y=A \sin (100 \pi t+3 x)$$. The distance between two points on the wave at a phase difference of $$\frac{\pi}{3}$$ radian is
explanation:
Equation of the given harmonic progressive wave $$y=A \sin (100 \pi t+3)$$ ...... (i)

General equation of a harmonic wave

$$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{A} \sin (\mathrm{wt}+\mathrm{kx})$$ ..... (ii)

From equations (i) and (ii), $$\omega=100 \pi, k=3$$

But, $$\mathrm{k}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Rightarrow 3=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}$$

$$\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$$

We also know,

Path difference $$\Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi} \times$$ Phase difference $$\Delta \phi$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \Delta x & =\frac{2 \pi}{3 \times 2 \pi} \times \frac{\pi}{3} \quad \ldots .\left(\text { Given } \Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\ & =\frac{\pi}{9} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$$

content: The amplitude of a particle executing S.H.M. is $$3 \mathrm{~cm}$$. The displacement at which its kinetic energy will be $$25 \%$$ more than the potential energy is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given }: \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{P} \cdot \mathrm{E}+\frac{25}{100} \cdot \mathrm{P} \cdot \mathrm{E} \\ & \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{P} \cdot \mathrm{E}+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{P} \cdot \mathrm{E} \\ & \text { We know K.E }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2\right) \text { and P.E }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{x}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{P} \cdot \mathrm{E} \\ & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2\right)=\frac{5}{4}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{x}^2\right) \\ & \mathrm{A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2=\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{x}^2 \\ & \mathrm{~A}^2=\frac{9}{4} \mathrm{x}^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~A}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{x} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \times \frac{2}{3}=2 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two S.H.Ms. are represented by equations $$\mathrm{y}_1=0.1 \sin \left(100 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$$ and $$\mathrm{y}_2=0.1 \cos (100 \pi \mathrm{t})$$ The phase difference between the speeds of the two particles is
explanation:
Given: $$\mathrm{y}_1=0.1 \sin \left(100 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$$ and

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{y}_2=0.1 \cos (100 \pi \mathrm{t}) & \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{v}_1=\frac{\mathrm{dy_{1 }}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =0.1 \times 100 \pi \cos \left(100 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\ \mathrm{v}_2=\frac{\mathrm{dy_{2 }}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-0.1 \times 100 \pi \sin (100 \pi \mathrm{t}) \\ & =(0.1 \times 100 \pi) \cos \left(100 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Phase difference of velocity of first particle with respect to the velocity of second particle at $$t=0$$ is

$$\therefore \quad \Delta \phi=\phi_1-\phi_2=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{-\pi}{6}$$

content: A spring has a certain mass suspended from it and its period for vertical oscillations is '$$T_1$$'. The spring is now cut in to two equal halves and the same mass is suspended from one of the halves. The period of vertical oscillations is now '$$\mathrm{T}_2$$'. The ratio $$\mathrm{T}_1 / \mathrm{T}_2$$ is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{T}_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}}} .$$

Also, spring constant $$(\mathrm{k}) \propto \frac{1}{\text { Length }(l)}$$

When the spring is half in length, then $$k$$ becomes twice.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{T}_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2 \mathrm{k}}} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\sqrt{2} \end{array}$$

content: A particle is vibrating in S.H.M. with an amplitude of $$4 \mathrm{~cm}$$. At what displacement from the equilibrium position is its energy half potential and half kinetic?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { K.E } & =\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(A^2-x^2\right) \text { and } \\ \text { P.E } & =\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2 \\ \therefore \quad \text { T.E } & =\text { K.E }+ \text { P.E } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(A^2-x^2\right)+\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \\ & \text { Given: K.E }=\text { P.E } \\ \therefore \quad A^2 & -x^2=x^2 \\ \therefore \quad & A^2=2 x^2 \\ \therefore \quad x & =\sqrt{\frac{A^2}{2}}=\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \therefore \quad x & =\frac{4}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \sqrt{2} ~\mathrm{c m} \end{aligned}$$

content: A rubber ball filled with water, having a small hole is used as the bob of a simple pendulum. The time period of such a pendulum

options.content: first increases and then decreases, finally having same value as at the beginning.

explanation:
From $$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

The effective length increases due to this flow of water. Therefore, $$\mathrm{T}$$ increases. As the water flows out, the length decreases and becomes equal to the original length. Hence, the time period becomes equal to the value at the beginning.

content: The maximum velocity of a particle performing S.H.M. is '$$\mathrm{V}$$'. If the periodic time is made $$\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^d$$ and the amplitude is doubled, then the new maximum velocity of the particle will be
explanation:
Given $$\mathrm{T}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~T}$$ and $$\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=2 \mathrm{~A}$$

$$\therefore \quad \omega^{\prime}=\frac{2 \pi}{T^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \pi}{\left(\frac{1}{3} T\right)}=\frac{6 \pi}{T}=3 \omega$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { The new maximum velocity } \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \mathrm{V}^{\prime} & =\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \omega^{\prime} \\ & =(2 \mathrm{~A}) \times(3 \omega) \\ & =6 \mathrm{~A} \omega \\ & =6 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The time taken by a particle executing simple harmonic motion of period '$$\mathrm{T}$$', to move from the mean position to half the maximum displacement is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{A} \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}\right) \text { where } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} / 2 \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=\mathrm{A} \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}\right) \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\sin \left(\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}\right) \\ & \therefore \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{12} \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a medium, the phase difference between two particles separated by a distance '$$x$$' is $$\left(\frac{\pi}{5}\right)^{\text {c }}$$. If the frequency of the oscillation of particles is $$25 \mathrm{~Hz}$$ and the velocity of propagation of the waves is $$75 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$, then the value of $$x$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{75}{25}=3 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \text { and phase difference }=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \text { (path difference) } \\ & \therefore \frac{\pi}{5}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \mathrm{x} \\ & \therefore 10 \mathrm{x}=3 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=\frac{3}{10}=0.3 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle starts oscillating simple harmonically from its mean position with time period '$$T$$'. At time $$t=\frac{T}{12}$$, the ratio of the potential energy to kinetic energy of the particle is $$\left(\sin 30^{\circ}=\cos 60^{\circ}=0.5, \cos 30^{\circ}=\sin 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3} / 2\right)$$
explanation:
The particle starts from the mean position.

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega \mathrm{t} & =\mathrm{A} \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}\right) \times \mathrm{t} \\ & =A \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}} \times \frac{\mathrm{T}}{12}\right) \\ & =A \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \quad \therefore \mathrm{x}^2=\frac{\mathrm{A}^2}{4}$$

$$\therefore$$ The particle is at a distance $$\mathrm{A} / 2$$ from the mean position.

At this point its P.E. $$=\frac{1}{2} K x^2=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2\quad \text{...... (1)}$$

and its K.E. $$=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2\right)\quad \text{..... (2)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{\text { P.E. }(\mathrm{U})}{\text { K.E. }(\mathrm{K})}=\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{A}^2}{4}}{\mathrm{~A}^2-\frac{\mathrm{A}^2}{4}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{A}^2}{4}}{\frac{3 \mathrm{~A}^2}{4}} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{K}}=\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The displacement of a particle performing S.H.M. is given by $$x=5 \sin (3 t+3)$$, where $$x$$ is in $$\mathrm{cm}$$ and $$t$$ is in second. The maximum acceleration of the particle will be
explanation:
To determine the maximum acceleration of a particle performing Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M.), we start with the given displacement function:

$$x = 5 \sin (3t + 3)$$

Here, $ x $ is in centimeters and $ t $ is in seconds. In S.H.M., the acceleration $ a $ is directly proportional to the displacement $ x $ and is given by:

$$a = -\omega^2 x$$

where $ \omega $ is the angular frequency. From the given displacement equation, we can see that the angular frequency $ \omega $ is 3. Thus, the maximum displacement $ x_{\text{max}} $ is 5 cm because the sine function's amplitude is 5.

The maximum acceleration $ a_{\text{max}} $ occurs when the sine function equals 1 (i.e., at maximum displacement). Hence, we can calculate the maximum acceleration as follows:

$$a_{\text{max}} = \omega^2 \cdot x_{\text{max}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$a_{\text{max}} = 3^2 \cdot 5$$

$$a_{\text{max}} = 9 \cdot 5$$

$$a_{\text{max}} = 45 \, \mathrm{cm} \, \mathrm{s}^{-2}$$

Thus, the maximum acceleration of the particle is:

Option C: $$45 \, \mathrm{cm} \, \mathrm{s}^{-2}$$

content: Two identical springs of constant '$$\mathrm{K}$$' are connected in series and parallel in shown in figure. A mass '$$\mathrm{M}$$' is suspended from them. The ratio of their frequencies is series to parallel combination will be
explanation:
In series combination, the effective spring constant

$$\mathrm{k}_1=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}$$

In the parallel combination, the effective spring constant is

$$\begin{aligned} k_2 & =2 k \\ f_1 & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_1}{m}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{2 m}} \\ f_2 & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_2}{m}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{2 k}{m}} \\ \therefore \frac{f_1}{f_2} & =\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle performing linear S.H.M. of amplitude $$0.1 \mathrm{~m}$$ has displacement $$0.02 \mathrm{~m}$$ and acceleration $$0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$$. The maximum velocity of the particle in $$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Acceleration } \mathrm{a}=\omega^2 \mathrm{x} \\ & \therefore \quad \omega^2=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{0.5}{0.02}=25 \\ & \therefore \quad \omega=5 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \quad \mathrm{V}_{\max }=\mathrm{A} \omega^2=0.1 \times 5=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

content: A body is executing S.H.M. under the action of force having maximum magntude $$50 \mathrm{~N}$$. When its energy is half kinetic and half potential; the magnitude of the force acting on the particle is
explanation:
Potential energy is a half of the total energy

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 x^2=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2\right] \\ & \therefore x^2=\frac{A^2}{2} \text { or } x=\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

Maximum force $$F_m=\omega^2 \mathrm{~A}$$

Force at a distance $$\mathrm{x}$$ is $$\mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\omega^2 \mathrm{x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{F^{\prime}}{F_m}=\frac{x}{A}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \therefore F^{\prime}=\frac{F_m}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{50}{\sqrt{2}}=25 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: A bob of simple pendulum of mass 'm' perform $$\mathrm{SHM}$$ with amplitude '$$\mathrm{A}$$' and period 'T'. Kinetic energy of pendulum of displacement $$x=\frac{A}{2}$$ will be
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Kinetic energy }=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(A^2-x^2\right) \\ & \text { At } x=\frac{A}{2}, \text { K.E. }=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(\frac{3}{4} A^2\right)=\frac{3}{8} m \omega^2 A^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}\right)^2 \cdot \mathrm{A}^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{~m} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^2}{\mathrm{~T}^2} \cdot \mathrm{A}^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{m \pi^2 A^2}{T^2} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: An object executes SHM along $$x$$-axis with amplitude $$0.06 \mathrm{~m}$$. At certain distance '$$\mathrm{x}$$' metre from mean position, it has kinetic energy $$10 \mathrm{~J}$$ and potential energy $$8 \mathrm{~J}$$. the distance '$$\mathrm{x}$$' will be
explanation:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the total energy (E) of the system remains constant and is the sum of its kinetic energy (K.E) and potential energy (P.E). Therefore, we have:

$$E = \text{K.E} + \text{P.E}$$

Given that the kinetic energy is $$10 \mathrm{~J}$$ and the potential energy is $$8 \mathrm{~J}$$, the total energy can be calculated as:

$$E = 10 \mathrm{~J} + 8 \mathrm{~J} = 18 \mathrm{~J}$$

The total energy in SHM can also be expressed in terms of amplitude (A) and the maximum potential energy which occurs at the extreme position. Hence, the total energy is given by:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} k A^2$$

where $ k $ is the force constant. Since the amplitude (A) is $$0.06 \mathrm{~m}$$, we can substitute the values to get:

$$18 \mathrm{~J} = \frac{1}{2} k (0.06)^2$$

Solving for $ k $, we get:

$$k = \frac{2 \times 18}{(0.06)^2}$$

$$k = \frac{36}{0.0036}$$

$$k = 10000 \mathrm{~N/m}$$

Now, using the given kinetic and potential energy values at a distance $ x $ from the mean position, we know that:

$$\text{K.E} = \frac{1}{2} k (A^2 - x^2)$$

$$\text{P.E} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

Given that:

$$10 \mathrm{~J} = \frac{1}{2} k (A^2 - x^2)$$

$$8 \mathrm{~J} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

First, let's solve for $ x $ from the potential energy equation:

$$8 = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

$$x^2 = \frac{16}{k}$$

Substituting $ k = 10000 \mathrm{~N/m} $:

$$x^2 = \frac{16}{10000}$$

$$x^2 = 0.0016 \mathrm{~m}^2$$

$$x = 0.04 \mathrm{~m}$$

Therefore, the distance '$$x$$' from the mean position where the kinetic energy is $$10 \mathrm{~J}$$ and the potential energy is $$8 \mathrm{~J}$$ is:

Option C: 0.04 m

content: A body executes SHM under the action of force '$$\mathrm{F}_1$$' with time period '$$\mathrm{T}_1$$'. If the force is changed to '$$\mathrm{F_2}$$', it executes SHM with period '$$\mathrm{T_2}$$'. If both the forces '$$\mathrm{F_1}$$' and '$$\mathrm{F}_2$$' act simultaneously in the same direction on the body, its time period is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{T}_1=\mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{x} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}}} \quad \mathrm{F}_2=\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{x}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}}} \quad \mathrm{F}=\left(\mathrm{K}_1+\mathrm{K}_2\right) \mathrm{x} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{T}_1^2 & =4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}_1} \quad \mathrm{~T}_2^2=4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}_2} \\ \mathrm{~T}^2 & =4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}_1+\mathrm{K}_2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}^2} & =\frac{\mathrm{K}_1+\mathrm{K}_2}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & =\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1^2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2^2} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2}{\mathrm{~T}_1^2 \mathrm{~T}_2^2} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{T}^2 & =\frac{\mathrm{T}_1^2 \mathrm{~T}_2^2}{\mathrm{~T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2} \\ \therefore \mathrm{T} & =\frac{\mathrm{T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle performing S.H.M. when displacement is '$$x$$', the potential energy and restoring force acting on it are denoted by '$$E$$' and '$$F$$' respectively. The relation between $$x, E$$ and $$F$$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \quad \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} k x^2 ; \quad \mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{kx} \\ & \therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{kx}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{F}}=-\mathrm{x} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{F}}+\mathrm{x}=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: A body is performing S.H.M. of amplitude 'A'. The displacement of the body from a point where kinetic energy is maximum to a point where potential energy is maximum, is
explanation:
The correct answer is Option B: $$ \pm A $$.

Here's why:

In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the total mechanical energy (the sum of kinetic and potential energy) remains constant. This means that when kinetic energy is at its maximum, potential energy is at its minimum, and vice-versa.

Let's break down the key points:

  • Kinetic Energy Maximum: This occurs when the body is passing through its equilibrium position. At this point, the velocity is maximum, and therefore kinetic energy is maximum.

  • Potential Energy Maximum: This occurs at the extreme ends of the motion, where the body momentarily comes to rest. At these points, the displacement from equilibrium is maximum, and hence potential energy is maximum.

  • Displacement: The displacement from the point of maximum kinetic energy (equilibrium) to the point of maximum potential energy (extreme position) is exactly equal to the amplitude 'A'.

Therefore, the displacement is $$ \pm A $$ (positive or negative depending on the direction of the motion).

content: A particle excuting S.H.M starts from the mean position. Its amplitude is 'A' and time period '$$\mathrm{T}$$' At what displacement its speed is one-fourth of the maximum speed?
explanation:
To determine the displacement at which the speed of the particle is one-fourth of its maximum speed, we need to understand the relationship between displacement, amplitude, and velocity in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM).

In SHM, the velocity $v$ of a particle at a displacement $x$ from the mean position is given by:

$$ v = \omega \sqrt{A^2 - x^2} $$

where:

  • $ \omega $ is the angular frequency, and
  • $ A $ is the amplitude.

The angular frequency $ \omega $ is related to the time period $ T $ by:

$$ \omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} $$

The maximum speed $ v_{max} $ occurs when the particle is at the mean position (i.e., $ x = 0 $), and it is given by:

$$ v_{max} = \omega A $$

We are given that the speed is one-fourth of the maximum speed. Therefore, we can write:

$$ \frac{v_{max}}{4} = \frac{\omega A}{4} $$

We need to find the displacement $ x $ at which this condition holds:

$$ \frac{\omega A}{4} = \omega \sqrt{A^2 - x^2} $$

We can divide both sides by $ \omega $:

$$ \frac{A}{4} = \sqrt{A^2 - x^2} $$

Next, we square both sides to eliminate the square root:

$$ \left(\frac{A}{4}\right)^2 = A^2 - x^2 $$

This simplifies to:

$$ \frac{A^2}{16} = A^2 - x^2 $$

Next, we rearrange the equation to solve for $ x $:

$$ x^2 = A^2 - \frac{A^2}{16} $$

$$ x^2 = \frac{16A^2 - A^2}{16} $$

$$ x^2 = \frac{15A^2}{16} $$

$$ x = \frac{A \sqrt{15}}{4} $$

Therefore, the displacement at which the speed is one-fourth of the maximum speed is:

Option D: $$ \frac{\mathrm{A} \sqrt{15}}{40} $$

content: A particle connected to the end of a spring executes S.H.M. with period '$$T_1$$'. While the corresponding period for another spring is '$$\mathrm{T}_2$$'. If the period of oscillation with two springs in series is 'T', then
explanation:
$$T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k_1}} \quad T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k_2}}$$

When the two springs are connected in series, the effective spring constant is given by

$$\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}_2}$$

When connected in series

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{T} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}}} \\ \therefore \mathrm{T}^2 & =4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}}=4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}_2}\right]=4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}_1}+4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{k}_2} \\ & =\mathrm{T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2 \\ \therefore \mathrm{T} & =\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2} \end{aligned}$$

content: '$$n$$' waves are produced on a string in 1 second. When the radius of the string is doubled, keeping tension same, the number of waves produced in 1 second for the same harmonic will be
explanation:
The frequency of a wave on a string depends on the tension in the string and the linear mass density (mass per unit length) of the string. The formula for the frequency, when we consider the tension $T$ and the linear mass density $\mu$, is given by:

$$f = \frac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

where:

  • $f$ is the frequency of the wave,

  • $L$ is the length of the vibrating segment of the string,

  • $T$ is the tension in the string,

  • $\mu$ is the linear mass density of the string ($\mu = \frac{m}{L}$, with $m$ being the mass and $L$ the length).

When the radius of the string is doubled, its cross-sectional area becomes four times larger (since the area of a circle is $A = \pi r^2$, doubling $r$ increases $A$ by a factor of 4). This implies that the mass of a given length of the string also becomes four times larger, and therefore, the linear mass density ($\mu$) increases by a factor of 4, because the length of the string we are examining hasn't changed.

Now, taking this into account, the new linear mass density is $4\mu$, and substituting this into the frequency formula gives us:

$$f_{new} = \frac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{4\mu}} = \frac{1}{2L} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} = \frac{1}{2} f$$

This shows that the frequency of the wave, and therefore the number of waves produced per second (since frequency and the number of waves per second are equivalent), will be halved if the radius of the string is doubled while keeping the tension the same. The correct answer is thus:

Option B $$\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}$$

content: A body of mass '$$m$$' performs linear S.H.M. given by equation $$x=P \sin \omega t+Q \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$. The total energy of the particle at any instant is
explanation:
To find the total energy of a particle performing Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M.), we start with the given equation of motion:

$$x = P \sin \omega t + Q \sin \left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$

First, we simplify the given equation using trigonometric identities. The identity for $$\sin \left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ can be rewritten as $$\cos \omega t$$ because $$\sin \left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = \cos \theta$$. Hence, the motion equation becomes:

$$x = P \sin \omega t + Q \cos \omega t$$

To find the total energy, we need to consider both kinetic and potential energies of the system. The total energy in S.H.M. is given by the sum of potential and kinetic energies, which remains constant and can be represented as:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2$$

where $$A$$ is the amplitude of the motion. In this case, the total amplitude of the motion given by the resultant of $$P \sin \omega t$$ and $$Q \cos \omega t$$ is actually the resultant vector magnitude of $$P$$ and $$Q$$, since they can be seen as perpendicular components (similar to a Pythagorean theorem scenario), and hence the resultant or effective amplitude $$A$$ is:

$$A = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$$

Thus, substituting $$A = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$$ into the equation for total energy, we get:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 (\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2})^2$$

$$E = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 (P^2 + Q^2)$$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option C: $$\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2\left(P^2+Q^2\right)$$

content: A mass $$0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$$ performs S.H.M. with a frequency $$\frac{16}{\pi} \mathrm{Hz}$$. At a certain displacement it has kinetic energy $$2 \mathrm{~J}$$ and potential energy $$1.2 \mathrm{~J}$$. The amplitude of oscillation is
explanation:
To determine the amplitude of the oscillation for the given simple harmonic motion (SHM), we first use some basic principles and formulas related to SHM.

Given:

  • Mass, $$m = 0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$$
  • Frequency, $$f = \frac{16}{\pi} \mathrm{Hz}$$
  • Kinetic energy, $$KE = 2 \mathrm{~J}$$ at a certain displacement
  • Potential energy, $$PE = 1.2 \mathrm{~J}$$ at the same displacement

First, the total energy in the SHM system is the sum of kinetic energy and potential energy:

$$E_{\text{total}} = KE + PE = 2 \mathrm{~J} + 1.2 \mathrm{~J} = 3.2 \mathrm{~J}$$

In SHM, the total energy is also given by the formula:

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2$$

where:

  • $$m$$ is the mass.
  • $$\omega$$ is the angular frequency.
  • $$A$$ is the amplitude of oscillation.

First, we need to find the angular frequency $$\omega$$ from the given frequency $$f$$:

$$\omega = 2\pi f$$

Substituting the given frequency, we get:

$$\omega = 2\pi \left(\frac{16}{\pi}\right) = 32 \mathrm{~rad/s}$$

Now, we use the total energy formula to find the amplitude $$A$$:

$$3.2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 32^2 \times A^2$$

Simplifying, we get:

$$3.2 = 0.2 \times 1024 \times A^2$$

$$3.2 = 204.8 A^2$$

$$A^2 = \frac{3.2}{204.8} = 0.015625$$

$$A = \sqrt{0.015625} = 0.125 \mathrm{~m}$$

Hence, the amplitude of oscillation is $$0.125 \mathrm{~m}$$.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B

$$0.125 \mathrm{~m}$$

content: If the amplitude of linear S.H.M. is decreased then

options.content: its period and total energy will increase.
its period will increase and total energy will decrease
its period and total energy will decrease.
its period will not change but total energy will decrease

explanation:
To answer this question, it's essential to understand the characteristics of Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M.) and the effects of amplitude on its various aspects.

Period of S.H.M.: The period of a simple harmonic oscillator is the time it takes for one complete cycle of motion. For a mass-spring system, the period $T$ is given by:

$$ T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} $$

where $m$ is the mass of the object and $k$ is the spring constant. Notice that the period $T$ depends only on the mass and the spring constant, and not on the amplitude $A$ of the oscillation. Thus, decreasing the amplitude does not affect the period of the motion.

Total Energy of S.H.M.: The total energy $E$ in simple harmonic motion is proportional to the square of its amplitude $A$. For a mass-spring system, the total energy is given by:

$$ E = \frac{1}{2} k A^2 $$

Given this relationship, if the amplitude is decreased, the total energy will also decrease.

Therefore, based on this analysis:

  • Option A: Incorrect. The period does not increase with a decrease in amplitude, and the total energy decreases rather than increases.
  • Option B: Incorrect. The period does not increase with a decrease in amplitude, though the total energy does decrease.
  • Option C: Incorrect. The period does not decrease with a decrease in amplitude, though the total energy does decrease.
  • Option D: Correct. The period remains unchanged, but the total energy decreases with a decrease in amplitude.

Hence, the correct answer is:

Option D: its period will not change but total energy will decrease.

content: A child is sitting on a swing which performs S.H.M. It has minimum and maximum heights from ground $$0.75 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and $$2 \mathrm{~m}$$ respectively. Its maximum speed will be $$\left[\mathrm{g}=10 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}^2}\right]$$
explanation:
Maximum potential energy is attained at the highest point which gets converted into kinetic energy at the lowest point.

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \mathrm{h}=2-0.75=1.25 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \quad \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\mathrm{mgh} \\ & \therefore \quad v^2=2 \mathrm{gh}=2 \times 10 \times 1.25=25 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{v}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

content: A pendulum clock is running fast. To correct its time, we should

options.content: reduce the mass of the bob.
increase the length of the pendulum.
reduce the length of the pendulum.

explanation:
The periodic time of a pendulum is given by

$$\mathrm{T=2\pi\sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}}$$

$$\therefore T \propto \sqrt l$$

Hence, to increase the periodic time, length has to be increased. The periodic time is independent of mass and amplitude.

content: A particle is performing S.H.M. with maximum velocity '$$v$$'. If the amplitude is tripled and periodic time is doubled then maximum velocity will be
explanation:
To solve this problem, we first need to recall the formula that relates the maximum velocity ($$v_{max}$$) of a particle performing simple harmonic motion (S.H.M.) to its amplitude ($$A$$) and its angular frequency ($$\omega$$). The formula for maximum velocity in S.H.M. is given by:

$$ v_{max} = \omega A $$

Now the angular frequency ($$\omega$$) is related to the period ($$T$$) of the S.H.M. by the relationship:

$$ \omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} $$

Thus, we can rewrite the expression for maximum velocity as:

$$ v_{max} = A \cdot \frac{2\pi}{T} $$

In the initial situation, let's denote the amplitude as $$A$$ and the periodic time as $$T$$. The maximum velocity given is $$v$$, so we can write:

$$ v = A \cdot \frac{2\pi}{T} $$

According to the question, the amplitude is tripled (making it $$3A$$), and the periodic time is doubled (making it $$2T$$). We need to find the new maximum velocity, let's call it $$v'$$:

$$ v' = (3A) \cdot \frac{2\pi}{(2T)} $$

Simplifying gives us:

$$ v' = 3A \cdot \frac{\pi}{T} $$

Now, if we recall the original maximum velocity $$v = A \cdot \frac{2\pi}{T}$$, we can express $$\frac{\pi}{T}$$ in terms of $$v$$ and $$A$$:

$$ \frac{\pi}{T} = \frac{v}{2A} $$

Substituting this into the expression for $$v'$$, we get:

$$ v' = 3A \times \frac{v}{2A} = \frac{3}{2}v = 1.5v $$

Therefore, the new maximum velocity when the amplitude is tripled and the periodic time is doubled is $$1.5v$$. The correct answer is:

Option A $$1.5 \mathrm{~v}$$

content: A particle executes S.H.M. of period $$\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}}$$ second along a straight line $$4 \mathrm{~cm}$$ long. The displacement of the particle at which the velocity is numerically equal to the acceleration is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{ZA}=4 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{A}=2 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & \text { Acceleration, } a=\omega^2 \mathrm{x} \\ & \text { Velocity, } v=\omega \sqrt{A^2-x^2} \\ & \therefore \quad \omega^2 \mathrm{x}=\omega \sqrt{A^2-\mathrm{x}^2} \\ & \omega x=\sqrt{A^2-x^2} \\ & \omega^2 x^2=A^2-x^2 \\ & \therefore \quad 3 \mathrm{x}^2=\mathrm{A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2 \quad\left(\therefore \omega=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}=\sqrt{3}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad 4 \mathrm{x}^2=\mathrm{A}^2 \\ & \mathrm{c}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle is suspended from a vertical spring which is executing S.H.M. of frequency $$5 \mathrm{~Hz}$$. The spring is unstretched at the highest point of oscillation. Maximum speed of the particle is $$(\mathrm{g} =10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2)$$
explanation:
At the highest point the particle will come to rest momentarily, hence it is at extreme position and has maximum force and acceleration. Since the spring is unstretched, the restoring forceto provided by the weight of the particle

$$\therefore \mathrm{mA} \omega^2=\mathrm{mg} $$

or $$A \omega^2=\mathrm{g}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore A & =\frac{g}{\omega^2} \\ \omega & =2 \pi f=2 \pi \times 5=10 \pi \\ \therefore A & =\frac{10}{100 \pi^2}=\frac{1}{10 \pi^2} \\ V_{\max } & =A \omega=\frac{1}{10 \pi^2} \times 10 \pi=\frac{1}{\pi}\end{aligned}$$

content: A body performs S.H.M. under the action of force '$$\mathrm{F}_1$$' with period '$$\mathrm{T}_1$$' second. If the force is changed to '$$\mathrm{F}_2$$' it performs S.H.M. with period '$$\mathrm{T_2}$$' second. If both forces '$$\mathrm{F_1}$$' and '$$\mathrm{F_2}$$' act simultaneously in the same direction on the body, the period in second will be
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}_1=\mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{F}_2=\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{F}=\left(\mathrm{K}_1+\mathrm{K}_2\right) \mathrm{x} \\ & \therefore T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{K}} \quad T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}_1^2=4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}_1} \quad \mathrm{~T}_2^2=4 \pi^2 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}_2} \\ & T^2=4 \pi^2 \frac{m}{K_1+K_2} \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_1+\mathrm{K}_2}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}} \quad=\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & =\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1^2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2^2}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2}{\mathrm{~T}_1^2 \mathrm{~T}_2^2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}^2=\frac{\mathrm{T}_1^2 \mathrm{~T}_2^2}{\mathrm{~T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_1^2+\mathrm{T}_2^2}} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: A mass '$$\mathrm{m}_1$$' is suspended from a spring of negligible mass. A spring is pulled slightly in downward direction and released, mass performs S.H.M. of period '$$\mathrm{T}_1$$'. If the mass is increased by '$$\mathrm{m}_2$$', the time period becomes '$$\mathrm{T}_2$$'. The ratio $$\frac{\mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1}$$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } \\ & T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m_1}{k}}, T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m_1+m_2}{k}} \\ & \therefore \frac{T_2}{T_1}=\sqrt{\frac{m_1+m_2}{m_1}} \\ & \therefore \frac{T_2^2}{T_1^2}=\frac{m_1+m_2}{m_1} \\ & \therefore \frac{T_2^2-T_1^2}{T_1^2}=\frac{m_2}{m_1} \end{aligned} $$

content:
Two particles $$\mathrm{P}$$ and $$\mathrm{Q}$$ performs S.H.M. of same amplitude and frequency along the same straight line. At a particular instant, maximum distance between two particles is $$\sqrt{2}$$ a. The initial phase difference between them is

$$\left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}\right]$$
explanation:
$$x=x_1+x_2=A \sin (\omega t+\alpha)$$

Maximum value of $$x_1+x_2=A=\sqrt{2} a$$

$$\begin{aligned} & A^2=a^2+a^2+2 a^2 \cos \alpha=2 a^2(1+\cos \alpha) \\ & \therefore 2 a^2=2 a^2(1+\cos \alpha) \quad \therefore 1=1+\cos \alpha \\ & \therefore \quad \cos \alpha=0 \quad \therefore \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle of mass 5kg is executing S.H.M. with an amplitude 0.3 m and time period $$\frac{\pi}{5}$$s. The maximum value of the force acting on the particle is
explanation:
m = 5 g = 5 $$\times$$ 10$$^{-3}$$ kg, A = 0.3 m, T = $$\frac{\pi}{5}$$s

Maximum force

F = m$$\omega^2$$A = 5 $$\times$$ 10$$^{-3} \times(10)^2\times0.3=0.15$$ N

content: Two bodies $A$ and $B$ of equal mass are suspended from two separate massles springs of force constant $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively. The bodies oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. The ratio of the amplitudes of body $A$ to that of body $B$ is
explanation:
The maximum velocity of body in SHM,

$$v=A \omega$$

where, $A$ is amplitude of body and $\omega$ is the angulat frequency.

It is given that, the maximum velocities of bodies are equal.

$$\begin{aligned} v_A & =v_B \\ A_A \omega_A & =A_B \omega_B \\ A_A \sqrt{\frac{k_1}{m}} & =A_B \sqrt{\frac{k_2}{m}} \quad\left(\because \omega=\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}\right) \\ \frac{A_A}{A_B} & =\sqrt{\frac{k_2}{k_1}} \end{aligned}$$

content: A bob of a simple pendulum has mass $m$ and is oscillating with an amplitude $a$. If the length of the pendulum is $L$, then the maximum tension in the string is $\left[\cos 0^{\circ}=1\right.$, $g=$ acceleration due to gravity]
explanation:
Consider the figure shown below

The string posses maximum tension when bob is at mean position of oscillation i.e., at position $R$.

From geometry, $O P=\sqrt{L^2-a^2}$

Also, $R P=O R-O P=L-\sqrt{L^2-a^2}$

The whole kinetic energy of bob at position $R$ is converted into its potential energy at position $B$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} m v^2 & =m g\left(L-\sqrt{L^2-a^2}\right) \\ v^2 & =2 g\left(L-\sqrt{L^2-a^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Balancing forces at } R \text {, } \\ & \qquad T-m g=\frac{m v^2}{I}=\frac{2 m g\left(L-\sqrt{L^2-a^2}\right)}{L} \\ & \therefore \quad T=m g+2 m g\left(1-\sqrt{1-\frac{a^2}{L^2}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Using approximation, $\sqrt{1-x^2}=1-\frac{x^2}{2}$ for $x<<1$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} T & =m g+2 m g\left[1-\left(1-\frac{a^2}{2^2}\right)\right] \\ & =m g+m g\left(\frac{a}{L}\right)^2=m g\left[1+\left(\frac{a}{L}\right)^2\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: A body of mass 64 g is made to oscillate turn by turn on two different springs $A$ and $B$. Spring $A$ and $B$ has force constant $4 \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and $16 \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ respectively. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ are period of oscillations of springs $A$ and $B$ respectively, then $\frac{T_1+T_2}{T_1-T_2}$ will be
explanation:
Given, $m=64 \mathrm{~g}=64 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$,

$$k_A=4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m} \text { and } k_B=16 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$$

The time period of oscillation of a spring,

$$\begin{aligned} & T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad T_A=T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k_A}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{4}} \\ & =2 \pi \sqrt{16 \times 10^{-3}} \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & \text { and } \quad T_B=T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k_B}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{64 \times 10^{-3}}{16}} \\ & =2 \pi \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-3}}\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get } \\ & \frac{T_1}{T_2}=\sqrt{\frac{16 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad T_1=2 T_2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{T_1+T_2}{T_1-T_2}=\frac{2 T_2+T_2}{2 T_2-T_2}=\frac{3 T_2}{T_2}=\frac{3}{1} \text { or } 3: 1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The damping force of an oscillator is directly proportional to the velocity. The unit of constant of proportionality is
explanation:
Given, damping force $$\propto$$ velocity

$$\begin{aligned} & F \propto v \\ & F=k v \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { where, } k \text { is the proportionality constant. }\\ &\Rightarrow \quad k=\frac{F}{v}=\frac{\mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2}{\mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{kg}}{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{kg} ~\mathrm{s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

content: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with period of 3 s . The time taken by it to cover a distance equal to half the amplitude from mean position is [$$\sin 30^{\circ}=0.5$$]
explanation:
The displacement of particle in SHM at any instant,

$$\begin{aligned} x & =A \sin \omega t \\ \frac{A}{2} & =A \sin \omega t \quad\left(\text { given, } x=\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ \sin \omega t & =\frac{1}{2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow \omega t=\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{lll} \Rightarrow & \frac{2 \pi}{T} \cdot t=\frac{\pi}{6} & \left(\because \omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2 \pi}{3} t=\frac{\pi}{6} & (\because T=3 \mathrm{~s}) \\ \Rightarrow & t=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~s} & \end{array}$$

content: A simple pendulum of length $$I$$ has a bob of mass $$m$$. It executes SHM of small amplitude A. The maximum tension in the string is ($$g=$$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Consider the figure shown below

The string possess maximum tension when bolls at mean position of oscillation i.e., at position $$R$$.

From geometry, $$O P=\sqrt{1^2-A^2}$$

Also, $$\quad R P=O R-O P=I-\sqrt{1^2-A^2}$$

The whole kinetic energy of bob at position $$R$$ is converted into its potential energy at position $$B$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} m v^2 & =m g\left(I-\sqrt{I^2-A^2}\right) \\ v^2 & =2 g\left(I-\sqrt{I^2-A^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Balancing forces at } R \text {, }\\ &\begin{aligned} T-m g & =\frac{m v^2}{I}=\frac{2 m g\left(I-\sqrt{l^2-A^2}\right)}{I} \\ \therefore \quad T & =m g+2 m g\left(1-\sqrt{1-\frac{A^2}{l^2}}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { Using approximation, } \sqrt{1-x^2}=1-\frac{x^2}{2} \text { for } x<<1 \text {, we }$$

$$\begin{aligned} T & =m g+2 m g\left[1-\left(1-\frac{A^2}{2^2}\right)\right] \\ & =m g+m g\left(\frac{A}{I}\right)^2 \\ & =m g\left[1+\left(\frac{A}{I}\right)^2\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: A block of mass $$m$$ attached to one end of the vertical spring produces extension $$x$$. If the block is pulled and released, the periodic time of oscillation is
explanation:
For vertical spring, the periodic time of oscillation is

$$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

As, restoring force, $$|F|=kx$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & k=\frac{F}{x}=\frac{m g}{x} \\ \therefore & T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{\frac{m g}{x}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{x}{g}} \end{array}$$

content: A simple pendulum of length $$L$$ has mass $$m$$ and it oscillates freely with amplitude $$A$$. At extreme position, its potential energy is ($$g=$$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
At extreme position, the potential energy of simple pendulum,

$$P E=\frac{1}{2} m A^2 \omega^2 \quad \text{.... (i)}$$

where, $$\omega=$$ angular frequency $$=\frac{2 \pi}{T}$$

For simple pendulum, $$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

$$\therefore \quad \omega=\frac{2 \pi}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}=\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}} \quad\left[\because T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}\right]$$

On putting value of $$\omega$$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$\mathrm{PE}=\frac{1}{2} m A^2\left(\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}\right)^2=\frac{m g A^2}{2 L}$$

content: For a particle performing SHM when displacement is $$x$$, the potential energy and restoring force acting on it is denoted by $$E$$ and $$F$$, respectively. The relation between $$x, E$$ and $$F$$ is
explanation:
For a particle performing Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the potential energy (PE) at a displacement $$x$$ from the equilibrium position is given by

$$E = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

where $$k$$ is the spring constant.

The restoring force $$F$$ acting on the particle is given by Hooke's law, which states that $$F = -kx$$. The negative sign indicates that the force is directed towards the equilibrium position, opposing the displacement.

To find the relation between $$x, E$$, and $$F$$, we can express $$k$$ from the equation of force:

$$F = -kx \Rightarrow k = -\frac{F}{x}$$

Substituting $$k$$ into the equation for potential energy:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \left(-\frac{F}{x}\right) x^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{F}{x} \cdot x^2$$

We simplify this to get:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}Fx$$

To find a relational formula involving $$E$$, $$F$$, and $$x$$, we can solve the equation $$E = \frac{1}{2}Fx$$ for one variable in terms of the others. However, let's examine the given options instead. Rearranging the equation for $$E$$ to isolate terms similar to the options given, we obtain:

$$2E = Fx$$

Dividing both sides of this equation by $$F$$ gives us:

$$\frac{2E}{F} = x$$

Rearranging the terms to match the given options, we get:

$$\frac{2E}{F} - x = 0$$

Therefore, the correct relation between $$x, E$$, and $$F$$ is given by Option B:

$$\frac{2E}{F}-x=0$$

content: A person measures a time period of a simple pendulum inside a stationary lift and finds it to be $T$. If the lift starts accelerating upwards with an acceleration $\left(\frac{g}{3}\right)$, the time period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum inside a stationary lift,

$$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

where, $I=$ length of string

and $g=$ gravitational acceleration

Acceleration of lift in upward direction,

$$a=\frac{g}{3}$$

$\therefore$ Time period of pendulum,

$$\begin{aligned} \text { d of pendulum, } \\ \begin{aligned} T^{\prime} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g+a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g+\frac{g}{3}}} \\ & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{3l}{4 g}}=2 \pi \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} T \text { [from Eq. (i)] } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: In damped SHM, the SI unit of damping constant is
explanation:
In damped SHM, damping force is proportional to the velocity of the oscillator.

i.e., $$F_d \propto v \Rightarrow F_d=b v$$

where, $b$ is a damping constant.

$\therefore$ Damping constant, $b=\frac{F_d}{v}$

SI unit of damping constant

$$\begin{aligned} b & =\frac{\text { SI unit of force }}{\text { SI unit of velocity }}=\frac{\mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m} \cdot \mathrm{~s}^{-2}}{\mathrm{~m} \cdot \mathrm{~s}^{-1}} \\ & =\mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{~s}^{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{kg}}{\mathrm{~s}} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the SI unit of damping constant is $\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{s}$.

content: The total energy of a simple harmonic oscillaior is proportional to
explanation:
Total energy of simple harmonic oscillator is given by

$$E=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2$$

where, $m=$ mass of body performing SHM, $\omega=$ angular velocity and $A=$ amplitude.

$$\therefore \quad E \propto A^2$$

content: A particle is performing a linear simple harmonic motion of amplitude ' $A$ '. When it is midway between its mean and extreme position, the magnitudes of its velocity and acceleration are equal. What is the periodic time of the motion?
explanation:
In linear simple harmonic motion, the velocity of particle is given by

$$v=\omega \sqrt{A^2-x^2}\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

where, $\omega=$ angular frequency

$A=$ maximum displacement of amplitude

and $\quad x=$ displacement from mean position.

The acceleration of a particle in simple harmonic motion, (SHM) is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & a=\omega^2 x \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & \text { Here, } \quad x=\frac{A}{2} \\ & \text { Also, } \quad v=a \quad\text{(given)}\\ & \omega \sqrt{\left(A^2-x^2\right)}=\omega^2 x \text { [from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get] } \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(A^2-\frac{A^2}{4}\right)}=\omega \times \frac{A}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3} A}{2}=\omega \times \frac{A}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \pi}{T}=\sqrt{3} \quad\left[\because \omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad T=\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two pendulums begin to swing simultaneously. The first pendulum makes nine full oscillations when the other makes seven. The ratio of the lengths of the two pendulums is
explanation:
As two pendulums begin to swing simultaneously, then

$$n_1 T_1=n_2 T_2\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

where, $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the number of oscillations of first and second pendulum respectively and $T_1$ and $T_2$ be their respective time periods.

The time period of simple pendulum is given by

$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$

where, $I=$ length of pendulum

and $g=$ acceleration due to gravity

$\Rightarrow \quad T^2 \propto l\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

So, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{T_1^2}{T_2^2}=\frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

Here, $n_1=9, n_2=7$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{(7)^2}{(9)^2}=\frac{49}{81}$$

Hence, the ratio of pendulum lengths $I_1: I_2=49: 81$.

content: If ' $x$ ', $v$ ' and ' $a$ ' denote the displacement, velocity and acceleration of a particle respectively executing SHM of periodic time $h$ then which one of the following does not change with time?
explanation:
The dimensions of given variables of SHM are as

Displacement, $[x]=\left[M^0 L T^0\right]$

Velocity, $$[\mathrm{v}]=\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]$$

Acceleration, $[a]=\left[M^0 L T^{-2}\right]$

and time period, $[T]=\left[M^0 L^0 T\right]$

Now, checking each option for these values

For option (a)

$$\frac{[a][T]}{[x]}=\frac{\left[M^0 L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^0 L^0 T\right]}{\left[M^0 L T^0\right]}=\left[M^0 L^0 T^{-1}\right]$$

As it depends on time, so change with it.

For option (b),

$$\begin{aligned} {[a][T]+2 \pi[v] } & =\left[M^0 L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^0 L^0 T\right]+\left[M^0 L^0 T^{-1}\right] \\ & =\left[M^0 L T^{-1}\right] \end{aligned}$$

It is also dependent on time and hence changes with it.

For option (c),

$$\frac{[a][T]}{[v]}=\frac{\left[M^0 L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^0 L^0 T\right]}{\left[M^0 L T^{-1}\right]}=\left[M^0 L^0 T^0\right]$$

As it is a constant having no dimension, so it does not change with time.

For option (d),

$$\begin{aligned} \text { [a] }[t]+4 \pi^2[d]^2 & =\left[M^\rho L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^0 L^0 T\right]+\left[M^0 L T^{-1}\right]^2 \\ & =\left[L T^{-1}\right]+\left[L^2 T^{-2}\right] \end{aligned}$$

As, the term is dependent on time, so changes with it.

Also, it is dimensionally incorrect.

Hence, option (c) is correct.

content: The quantity which does not vary periodically for a particle performing SHM is
explanation:
For a particle executing simple harmonic motion (SHM),

Total energy $=$ kinetic energy + potential energy

$$\begin{aligned} & =E_K+E_P=\frac{1}{2} m v^2+\frac{1}{2} k x^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{m}\left(A^2-x^2\right)+\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \\ & (\because v=\sqrt{A^2-x^2} \text { where, } A \text { is the } \\ & \text { amplitude of the particle) } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} K A^2 \end{aligned}$$

It is independent of $t, x$ is remains constant i.e., it does not vary periodically.

However, the displacement of a particle executing SHM along $x$-axis is given by

$$x=A \sin (\omega t+\phi)$$

(where, $A=$ amplitude, $\omega=$ angular velocity $t=$ time, $\phi=$ initial phase of the particle)

i.e., displacement varies periodically with time $t$. velocity of particles executing SHM,

$$v=\frac{d x}{d t}=\omega \sqrt{A^2-x^2}$$

which varies with $x$.

Acceleration of the particle,

$$a=\frac{d v}{d t}=-\omega^2 x$$

Which also varies with $x$.

content: A particle executes the simple harmonic motion with an amplitude ' $A$ '. The distance travelled by it in one periodic time is
explanation:
Key Idea One time period means the total time in which the particle returns to the same point and in the same direction from where it was released.

Now, in simple harmonic motion (SHM) total distance travelled is 4A.

This is because the particle covers $A$ in $\frac{T}{4}$ time period i.e., when it moves from $A$ to $B$, So in complete $T$ time it covers 4 times the amplitude distance i.e., $4 A$.
